{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103","id":2080309110,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc57_v92","number":3103,"title":"Legacy non-standard character variable declaration","user":{"login":"ivan-pi","id":21085643,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDg1NjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/21085643?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi","html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-01-13T13:49:22Z","updated_at":"2024-01-13T13:49:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE taken from sl12f (lines 117 and 123 in https://netlib.org/aicm/sl12f.f)

```fortran
      SUBROUTINE sl12f()
C ALTERNATIVE 1
      CHARACTER*6 srname1
      CHARACTER*80 rec1(3)
C ALTERNATIVE 2
      CHARACTER srname2*6
      CHARACTER rec2(3)*80
C ALTERNATIVE 3
      CHARACTER(6) srname3
      CHARACTER(80) rec3(3)
C ALTERNATIVE 4
      CHARACTER(len=6) srname4
      CHARACTER(len=80) rec4(3)
      END
```

```txt
$ lfortran --version --fixed-form --implicit-interface -c sl12f_mwe1.f 
LFortran version: 0.30.0
Platform: Linux
Default target: x86_64-conda-linux-gnu
$ lfortran --fixed-form --implicit-interface -c sl12f_mwe1.f 
warning: Assuming implicit save attribute for variable declaration
 --> sl12f_mwe1.f:6:17
  |
6 |       CHARACTER srname2*6
  |                 ^^^^^^^^^ help: add explicit save attribute or initialize in a separate statement

warning: Assuming implicit save attribute for variable declaration
 --> sl12f_mwe1.f:7:17
  |
7 |       CHARACTER rec2(3)*80
  |                 ^^^^^^^^^^ help: add explicit save attribute or initialize in a separate statement
```
All of the four alternatives are accepted by other compilers:
```
$ ifort sl12f_mwe1.f -c 
$ nvfortran sl12f_mwe1.f -c
$ gfortran sl12f_mwe1.f -c
$ 
```

Oracle's [Fortran 77 Language Reference](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19957-01/805-4939/6j4m0vn7r/index.html) lists a fifth non-standard syntax:
```fortran
       CHARACTER A*17, B*17(3,4), V*17(9)	 nonstandard
```

It is accepted by ifort and nvfortran, but rejected by gfortran:
```
$ gfortran sl12f_mwe1.f -c
sl12f_mwe1.f:16:26:

   16 |       CHARACTER A*17, B*17(3,4), V*17(9)
      |                          1
Error: Syntax error in data declaration at (1)
$ gfortran -std=legacy sl12f_mwe1.f -c
sl12f_mwe1.f:16:26:

   16 |       CHARACTER A*17, B*17(3,4), V*17(9)
      |                          1
Error: Syntax error in data declaration at (1)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

J. Cody (see for example `power.f` in https://netlib.org/elefunt/). Since the routines were implemented in 1979, I guess the standard character type introduced in Fortran 77 was not yet widespread.

Here's another MWE:
```fortran
      INTEGER IOUT
      IOUT = 6
      WRITE (IOUT,1000)
      WRITE (IOUT,1100)
      STOP
 1000 FORMAT(20H1TEST OF X**1.0 VS X  //)
 1100 FORMAT(25H THIS CONCLUDES THE TESTS )
      END
```

It already works when switched to:
```fortran
      INTEGER IOUT
      IOUT = 6
      WRITE (IOUT,1000)
      WRITE (IOUT,1100)
      STOP
 1000 FORMAT("1TEST OF X**1.0 VS X "//)
 1100 FORMAT(" THIS CONCLUDES THE TESTS")
      END
```
Some of rules concerning Hollerith constants can be found in the Oracle [Fortran 77 Language Reference](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19957-01/805-4939/z40007365eaa/index.html)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/1890470583/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103","id":2080309110,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc57_v92","number":3103,"title":"Legacy non-standard character variable declaration","user":{"login":"ivan-pi","id":21085643,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDg1NjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/21085643?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi","html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-01-13T13:49:22Z","updated_at":"2024-01-13T14:41:07Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE taken from sl12f (lines 117 and 123 in https://netlib.org/aicm/sl12f.f)

```fortran
      SUBROUTINE sl12f()
C ALTERNATIVE 1
      CHARACTER*6 srname1
      CHARACTER*80 rec1(3)
C ALTERNATIVE 2
      CHARACTER srname2*6
      CHARACTER rec2(3)*80
C ALTERNATIVE 3
      CHARACTER(6) srname3
      CHARACTER(80) rec3(3)
C ALTERNATIVE 4
      CHARACTER(len=6) srname4
      CHARACTER(len=80) rec4(3)
      END
```

```txt
$ lfortran --version --fixed-form --implicit-interface -c sl12f_mwe1.f 
LFortran version: 0.30.0
Platform: Linux
Default target: x86_64-conda-linux-gnu
$ lfortran --fixed-form --implicit-interface -c sl12f_mwe1.f 
warning: Assuming implicit save attribute for variable declaration
 --> sl12f_mwe1.f:6:17
  |
6 |       CHARACTER srname2*6
  |                 ^^^^^^^^^ help: add explicit save attribute or initialize in a separate statement

warning: Assuming implicit save attribute for variable declaration
 --> sl12f_mwe1.f:7:17
  |
7 |       CHARACTER rec2(3)*80
  |                 ^^^^^^^^^^ help: add explicit save attribute or initialize in a separate statement
```
All of the four alternatives are accepted by other compilers:
```
$ ifort sl12f_mwe1.f -c 
$ nvfortran sl12f_mwe1.f -c
$ gfortran sl12f_mwe1.f -c
$ 
```

Oracle's [Fortran 77 Language Reference](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19957-01/805-4939/6j4m0vn7r/index.html) lists a fifth non-standard syntax:
```fortran
       CHARACTER A*17, B*17(3,4), V*17(9)	 nonstandard
```

It is accepted by ifort and nvfortran, but rejected by gfortran:
```
$ gfortran sl12f_mwe1.f -c
sl12f_mwe1.f:16:26:

   16 |       CHARACTER A*17, B*17(3,4), V*17(9)
      |                          1
Error: Syntax error in data declaration at (1)
$ gfortran -std=legacy sl12f_mwe1.f -c
sl12f_mwe1.f:16:26:

   16 |       CHARACTER A*17, B*17(3,4), V*17(9)
      |                          1
Error: Syntax error in data declaration at (1)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/1890476154","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103#issuecomment-1890476154","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3103","id":1890476154,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc5wrmB6","user":{"login":"ivan-pi","id":21085643,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDg1NjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/21085643?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi","html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-13T14:41:06Z","updated_at":"2024-01-13T14:41:06Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Note, this also caries over to character dummy arguments:
```
      subroutine MFEOUT (M,NAMES)
      integer M
      character NAMES(M)*(*)
      end
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/1890476154/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3104","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3104/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3104/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3104/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3104","id":2080381985,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc58ABwh","number":3104,"title":"Lawson & Hanson's least squares routines","user":{"login":"ivan-pi","id":21085643,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDg1NjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/21085643?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi","html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-01-13T16:37:34Z","updated_at":"2024-01-13T16:37:34Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The classic library of routines by Lawson & Hanson (https://netlib.org/lawson-hanson/) was published in the book Solving Least Squares Problems (https://epubs.siam.org/doi/book/10.1137/1.9781611971217). Until SciPy version 1.11 the [NNLS](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/v1.11.4/scipy/optimize/__nnls/nnls.f) routine was still used in SciPy, but [will be replaced](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/18570) with a different algorithm in version 1.12. 

Current compilation status of the 15 Lawson & Hanson routines with LFortran:
- [x] BNDACC
- [x] BNDSOL
- [x] DIFF
- [x] G1
- [x] G2
- [x] GEN
- [x] H12
- [ ] HFTI
- [ ] LDP
- [x] MFEOUT
- [ ] NNLS
- [ ] QRBD
- [x] SVA
- [x] SVDRS
- [ ] BVLS

I've been trying to compile them with LFortran:
```
[  6%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/bndacc.f.o
[  6%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/bndsol.f.o
[  6%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/diff.f.o
[  6%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/g1.f.o
[  6%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/g2.f.o
[  6%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/gen.f.o
[  6%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/h12.f.o
[  6%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/mfeout.f.o
[  7%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/sva.f.o
[  7%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/svdrs.f.o
[  7%] Linking Fortran static library liblawson-hanson.a
```

The routines `HFTI`, `LDP`, `NNLS`, and `QRBD` all fail because in an [arithmetic if](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19957-01/805-4939/6j4m0vn9p/index.html) statement:

```
$ make lawson-hanson -k
Scanning dependencies of target lawson-hanson
[  0%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/hfti.f.o
semantic error: No matching signature found for intrinsic diff
  --> /home/ivan/fortran/netlib/lawson-hanson/hfti.f:35:14
   |
35 |           IF(DIFF(HMAX+FACTOR*H(LMAX),HMAX)) 20,20,50   
   |              ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 


Note: Please report unclear or confusing messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
make[3]: *** [lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/build.make:154: lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/hfti.f.o] Error 1
[  0%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/ldp.f.o
semantic error: No matching signature found for intrinsic diff
  --> /home/ivan/fortran/netlib/lawson-hanson/ldp.f:66:11
   |
66 |       IF (DIFF(ONE+FAC,ONE)) 130,130,70 
   |           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 


Note: Please report unclear or confusing messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
make[3]: *** [lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/build.make:167: lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/ldp.f.o] Error 1
[  0%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/nnls.f.o
semantic error: No matching signature found for intrinsic diff
   --> /home/ivan/fortran/netlib/lawson-hanson/nnls.f:128:11
    |
128 |       IF (DIFF(UNORM+ABS(A(NPP1,J))*FACTOR,UNORM) .gt. ZERO) then
    |           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 


Note: Please report unclear or confusing messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
make[3]: *** [lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/build.make:193: lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/nnls.f.o] Error 1
[  0%] Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/lawson-hanson.dir/qrbd.f.o
semantic error: No matching signature found for intrinsic diff
  --> /home/ivan/fortran/netlib/lawson-hanson/qrbd.f:75:16
   |
75 |             IF(DIFF(DNORM+Q(K),DNORM) .ne. ZERO) go to 50
   |                ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 
```

A MWE is:
```fortran
      double precision FUNCTION DIFF(X,Y)
      double precision X, Y
      DIFF=X-Y  
      RETURN
      END

      subroutine hfti(a)
      double precision a, x, y
      double precision diff
      external diff
      x = 2.0d0
      y = 1.0d0
      if (diff(x,y)) 10,10,20 
   10 print *, "negative or zero"
      go to 30
   20 print *, "positive"
   30 return
      end
```


The `BVLS` function fails because of a scoping error: 
```
$ make bvls
Building Fortran object lawson-hanson/CMakeFiles/bvls.dir/bvls.f90.o
tokenizer error: ICE: Cannot recognize global scope entity
 --> /home/ivan/fortran/netlib/lawson-hanson/bvls.f90:1:1
  |
1 | SUBROUTINE BVLS (A, B, BND, X, RNORM, NSETP, W, INDEX, IERR)
  | ^ 
```
I'll comment once I have a MWE.

```
[ 10%] Linking Fortran executable simann
/usr/bin/ld: CMakeFiles/simann.dir/simann/simann.f.o: in function `sa':

If I replace line 645 in `simann.f`, containing

with

```
then it compiles succesfully:

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3110","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3110/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3110/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3110/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3110","id":2080480741,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc58AZ3l","number":3110,"title":"Consecutive common block syntax - semantic error","user":{"login":"ivan-pi","id":21085643,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDg1NjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/21085643?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi","html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-01-13T21:23:30Z","updated_at":"2024-01-13T21:23:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE based upon the Netlib code praxis (https://netlib.org/opt/praxis):

```fortran
      REAL*8 FUNCTION PRAXIS()
      COMMON /GLOBAL/ FX,LDT,DMIN,NF,NL
     .       /Q/ V,Q0,Q1,QA,QB,QC,QD0,QD1,QF1
      RETURN
      END
      SUBROUTINE MIN()
      COMMON /GLOBAL/ FX,LDT,DMIN,NF,NL
     .       /Q/ V,Q0,Q1,QA,QB,QC,QD0,QD1,QF1
      RETURN
      END
      REAL*8 FUNCTION FLIN (N,J,L,F,X,NF)
      COMMON /Q/ V,Q0,Q1,QA,QB,QC,QD0,QD1,QF1
      RETURN
      END
      SUBROUTINE QUAD()
      COMMON /GLOBAL/ FX,LDT,DMIN,NF,NL
     .       /Q/ V,Q0,Q1,QA,QB,QC,QD0,QD1,QF1
      RETURN
      END
      SUBROUTINE PRINT()
      COMMON /GLOBAL/ FX,LDT,DMIN,NF,NL
      RETURN
      END

```
Fails with:
```txt
$ lfortran -c --implicit-typing --fixed-form praxis_mwe1.f
semantic error: The order of variables in common block must be same in all programs
 --> praxis_mwe1.f:7:7 - 8:45
  |
7 |          COMMON /GLOBAL/ FX,LDT,DMIN,NF,NL
  |          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^...
...
  |
8 |         .       /Q/ V,Q0,Q1,QA,QB,QC,QD0,QD1,QF1
  | ...^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 
```


The syntax with consecutive common blocks,
```fortran
       COMMON [/[cb]/] nlist [[,]/[cb]/ nlist] 
```
is described in Oracles's [Fortran 77 Language Reference](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19957-01/805-4939/6j4m0vn7v/index.html).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3110/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3110/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3111","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3111/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3111/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3111/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3111","id":2080484463,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc58Aaxv","number":3111,"title":"Common block preceding declaration - semantic error","user":{"login":"ivan-pi","id":21085643,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDg1NjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/21085643?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi","html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-01-13T21:38:09Z","updated_at":"2024-01-13T21:38:09Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE based upon hopdm from Netlib (https://netlib.org/opt/hopdm.shar, note the link will download a shell archive):

```
C *** COMMON ARREAS
C     Cholesky factorization parameters.
      COMMON /CHFCT/   CSMALL,PIVTOL,TAU,DENSE,PIVMAX,PIVMIN,IDNSRW
      DOUBLE PRECISION CSMALL,PIVTOL,TAU,DENSE,PIVMAX,PIVMIN
      INTEGER*4        IDNSRW
      END
```

```txt
$ lfortran -c --implicit-typing --fixed-form hopdm_mwe1.f 
semantic error: Symbol is already declared in the same scope
 --> hopdm_mwe1.f:4:24
  |
4 |       DOUBLE PRECISION CSMALL,PIVTOL,TAU,DENSE,PIVMAX,PIVMIN
  |                        ^^^^^^ redeclaration
  |
3 |       COMMON /CHFCT/   CSMALL,PIVTOL,TAU,DENSE,PIVMAX,PIVMIN,IDNSRW
  |                        ~~~~~~ original declaration
```

It works if the common block is placed after the declarations. Other compilers accept it in both ways:
```
$ nvfortran hopdm_mwe1.f 
$ ifort hopdm_mwe1.f 
$ gfortran hopdm_mwe1.f
$
```

100     CONTINUE

```fortran
      DOUBLE PRECISION FUNCTION dstrem(z)
      IMPLICIT DOUBLE PRECISION (a-h,o-p,r-z),INTEGER (i-n),LOGICAL (q)
      dstrem = z
      END
```

```
$ lfortran -c --implicit-typing --fixed-form dcdflib_1.f 
syntax error: Token ',' is unexpected here
 --> dcdflib_1.f:2:46
  |
2 |       IMPLICIT DOUBLE PRECISION (a-h,o-p,r-z),INTEGER (i-n),LOGICAL (q)
  |                                              ^ 
```

A temporary workaround is to separate these into individual statements:
```fortran
      DOUBLE PRECISION FUNCTION dstrem(z)
      IMPLICIT DOUBLE PRECISION (a-h,o-p,r-z)
      IMPLICIT INTEGER (i-n)
      IMPLICIT LOGICAL (q)
      dstrem = z
      END
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3113/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3113/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fftpack/issues/34","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fftpack","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fftpack/issues/34/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fftpack/issues/34/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fftpack/issues/34/events","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/fftpack/issues/34","id":1603530058,"node_id":"I_kwDOAH8les5fk-1K","number":34,"title":"Improve interface of discrete cosine transforms","user":{"login":"cval26","id":52826240,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUyODI2MjQw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/52826240?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/cval26","html_url":"https://github.com/cval26","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cval26/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cval26/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cval26/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cval26/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cval26/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cval26/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cval26/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cval26/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cval26/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2023-02-28T17:49:22Z","updated_at":"2024-01-15T11:06:53Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Related to #9, #22. 
EDIT: See PR #35 for the implementation of item 1.
EDIT: See PR #38 for the implementation of item 2.

I suggest the following changes, which I argue will bring the interface of the FFTPACK cosine transforms in line with modern terminology, and thus make the interface easier to understand and use. I am certainly willing to implement those changes myself, I just post them here first to gather feedback.

The most common discrete cosine transforms (DCT) are types 1, 2, 3 and 4. Up to a scaling factor, the FFTPACK procedures

- `dcost` and modern `dct`correspond to the DCT type-1 (DCT-I);
- `dcosqb` and modern `iqct` correspond to DCT type-2 (DCT-II);
- `dcosqf` and modern `qct`correspond to DCT type-3 (DCT-III).

Note also that DCT-I is the unnormalized inverse of itself; while DCT-III is the unnormalized inverse of DCT-II, and conversely. [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discrete_cosine_transform#Formal_definition) has a nice summary of the definitions of the most common types of DCT.

Because FFTPACK calls the DCT types 2 & 3 transforms quarter-wave transforms, it's difficult for a new user of the library (like me) to find out what they correspond to; the quarter terminology is not in use anymore. This is also why @zoziha in #9 couldn't find their corresponding transforms in `scipy`.

Based on the above, I propose the following changes. 
- [x] Combine the modern interfaces `dct(x,n)` and `qct(x,n)` under one interface `dct(x,n,type)` where `type=1,2,3` (and similarly for the inverse ones). In this way, `dct(x,n,type=1)` corresponds to the DCT-I of the input data, and similarly for the others. This is the way the cosine transforms are also handled in `scipy.fft`. This will have no performance overhead, it's simply combining the two existing interfaces so that they're in line with current terminology. 
- [x] Create an interface to overload the existing in-place DCT subroutines, so that they can be called with names based on their type. For example, create a subroutine `dct_t1` for DCT-I that simply overloads the existing `dcost`, and similarly for DCT types 2, 3. See comments below for code example; thanks to @zoziha for the suggestion. 
- [ ] Write the definition formulas of the DCTs in LaTeX in the documentation.

These changes will allow us to potentially include more sine/cosine transforms in the future consistently, without having to break the interface later on. They will also allow us to improve the documentation to make it clear what each procedure computes. 

I am willing to implement the above and change the documentation and tests accordingly.

We should also change the DST interface in the analogous way in a standalone PR for consistency.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fftpack/issues/34/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fftpack/issues/34/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"reopened"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fftpack/issues/comments/1891929002","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/fftpack/issues/34#issuecomment-1891929002","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fftpack/issues/34","id":1891929002,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAH8les5wxIuq","user":{"login":"ivan-pi","id":21085643,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDg1NjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/21085643?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi","html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-15T11:06:53Z","updated_at":"2024-01-15T11:06:53Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"In case anyone is interested - Apple has a DCT method for the default float type in their Accelerate framework: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/accelerate/discrete_cosine_transforms?language=objc

The interface in Fortran would look something like this:
```
integer(c_int), parameter :: DCT_II = 2
integer(c_int), parameter :: DCT_III = 3
integer(c_int), parameter :: DCT_IV = 4

interface
    function DCT_CreateSetup(previous,length,type) &
            bind(c,name="vDSP_DCT_CreateSetup")
        type(c_ptr), value :: previous
        integer(c_long), value :: length
        integer(c_int), value :: type
    end function
    subroutine DCT_Execute(setup,input,ouput) &
            bind(c,name="vDSP_DCT_Execute")
        type(c_ptr), value :: setup
        real(c_float), intent(in) :: input(*)
        real(c_float), intent(out) :: output(*)
    end subroutine
    subroutine DFT_DestroySetup(setup) &
            bind(c,name="vDSP_DFT_DestroySetup")
        type(c_ptr), value :: setup
    end subroutine
end interface
```
The `DCT_Execute` routine can be used in-place (a Fortran wrapper with a single dummy argument would be needed to map the `intent` attribute correctly). There are some restrictions on the sizes.

It could be nice to compare performance of the FFTPACK DCT, versus the one in Accelerate. This could go to the Fortran-Lang benchmarks repository, see: https://github.com/fortran-lang/benchmarks/issues/18","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fftpack/issues/comments/1891929002/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Motivation

As linear algebra support develops, it would be useful to have simple interfaces to access test matrices to/from standard formats into the stdlib formats. 

### Prior Art

- Matrix Market: https://math.nist.gov/MatrixMarket/ https://math.nist.gov/MatrixMarket/mmio/f/mmiof77.html
- Harwell-Boeing: https://math.nist.gov/MatrixMarket/formats.html#hb-code
- NumPy: https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.load.html
- https://help.imsl.com/java/5.0.1/api/com/imsl/math/SparseMatrixEx2.html
- https://help.imsl.com/c/7.0/html/cmath/default.htm?turl=matrixstoragemodes.htm

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3108","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3108/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3108/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3108/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3108","id":2080470620,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc58AXZc","number":3108,"title":"Missing GNU extensions FLOAT and DFLOAT","user":{"login":"ivan-pi","id":21085643,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDg1NjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/21085643?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi","html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-01-13T20:48:21Z","updated_at":"2024-01-17T14:46:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE based upon an FFT routine from netlib (https://www.netlib.org/misc/fft.f):

```fortran
      SUBROUTINE FFT_MWE1(K)
      IMPLICIT INTEGER (A-Z)
      REAL RE, IM
      COMPLEX U(16)
      U(1) = CMPLX(RE, -SIGN(IM, REAL(K)))
      U(2) = CMPLX(RE, -SIGN(IM, FLOAT(K)))
      RETURN
      END
```

```txt
$ lfortran -c --implicit-typing --fixed-form fft_mwe1.f 
semantic error: Type mismatch in statement function: the second argument must have the same type and kind as the first argument.
 --> fft_mwe1.f:6:34
  |
6 |       U(2) = CMPLX(RE, -SIGN(IM, FLOAT(K)))
  |                                  ^^^^^^^^ 
```

The `FLOAT` function is a GNU extension. More information about extensions can be found in the Flang intrinsics document: https://flang.llvm.org/docs/Intrinsics.html","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3108/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3108/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/1895961385","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3108#issuecomment-1895961385","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/3108","id":1895961385,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc5xAhMp","user":{"login":"ivan-pi","id":21085643,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDg1NjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/21085643?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi","html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-17T14:46:25Z","updated_at":"2024-01-17T14:46:25Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"One restriction is that `float` only accepts integer arguments. 

From the flang intrinsic table;
```
FLOAT(INTEGER I) = REAL(I)
IDINT(DOUBLE PRECISION A) = INT(A)
IFIX(REAL A) = INT(A)
SNGL(DOUBLE PRECISION A) = REAL(A)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/1895961385/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

gfortran supports two more intrinsic modules, `omp_lib_kinds`, and `openacc`, which could be added here. 

I haven't checked what other compilers support. I'm certain Intel Fortran doesn't support `openacc`, whereas Cray (HPE) `fc` and `nvfortran` do. In any case it's probably best to leave the compiler raise an error about an unsupported intrinsic module.

```fortran
! dispatch_test.f90 --
!   Dispatch table using procedure pointers
module dispatch
implicit none
private
public :: table
abstract interface
    subroutine void()
    end subroutine
end interface
! Procedure container
type :: pp
    procedure(void), pointer, nopass :: pf => null()
end type
! Dispatch table
type(pp), parameter :: table(3) = [pp(a),pp(b),pp(c)]
contains
    subroutine a()
    print *, "A"
    end subroutine
    subroutine b()
    print *, "B"
    end subroutine
    subroutine c()
    print *, "C"
    end subroutine
end module

program test
use dispatch
implicit none
integer :: i
do i = 1, 3
call table(i)%pf()
end do
end program
```

It is supposed to be valid F2008 according to the NAG compiler:
```

Describe your issue.

While writing a cpp wrapper for scipy, the following warning was shown due to the very old Fortran coding style used in the fitpack part in interpolation.
```
interpolate/fitpack/*.f
```

Warning: Fortran 2018 deleted feature: Shared DO termination

This can easily be solved. E.g.:

```
      do 20 i=1,k1
         t(i) = xb
         t(j) = xe
         j = j-1
  20  continue
  ```
  
must be changed to 

```
      do i=1,k1
         t(i) = xb
         t(j) = xe
         j = j-1
      end do
```


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
none
```


### Error message

```shell
Warning: Warning: Fortran 2018 deleted feature: Shared DO termination
```

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/19557/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/19557/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/1824691168","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/19557#issuecomment-1824691168","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/19557","id":1824691168,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc5swpPg","user":{"login":"ivan-pi","id":21085643,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDg1NjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/21085643?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi","html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-11-23T16:25:07Z","updated_at":"2023-11-23T16:25:07Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"The code snippet you've shown, and that @lucascolley has linked, has no shared termination.

Shared termination refers to the case where nested loops end with the same continue statement:

```fortran
C shared_do.f
C
      do 10 i = 1, 3
      do 10 j = 1, 3
      write(*,*) i, j
c >>> SHARED TERMINATION <<<
   10 continue

      do 40 i = 1, 3
      do 50 j = 1, 3
      write(*,*) i, j
   50 continue
   40 continue
      end   
```

Only the first case will raise a warning:
```
$ gfortran shared_do.f 
shared_do.f:4:72:

    4 |       do 10 j = 1, 3
      |                                                                        1
Warning: Fortran 2018 deleted feature: Shared DO termination label 10 at (1)
```

So if the warning bothers you, you can fix it relatively easily by adding an explicit continue statement. The labelled do loop remains valid F2018 syntax (albeit marked as obsolescent).

,"html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-09-22T06:41:07Z","updated_at":"2023-09-22T06:41:07Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"The basic setup is actually quite simple. To serve a local instance, you just need to modify the file `etc/config/fortran.local.properties` in the Compiler Explorer main folder, by adding the following

```
compilers=lfortran:wasmlfortran

compiler.lfortran.name=LFortran
compiler.lfortran.exe=/usr/local/Caskroom/miniconda/base/envs/lf/bin/lfortran
compiler.lfortran.versionFlag=--version
compiler.lfortran.versionRe=\d+(\.\d+)+
compiler.lfortran.intelAsm=
compiler.lfortran.options=--generate-object-code
compiler.supportsBinary=true
compiler.supportsBinaryObject=true
compiler.lfortran.supportsExecute=true
```

It would be nice to also have WASM version. I've also noticed some compilers are able to show the AST or LLVM IR, but I haven't figured yet how to do it.

![image](https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/assets/21085643/516bcfbe-02bc-47a4-9781-ab333e86205f)

base"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":18,"created_at":"2023-05-27T09:25:20Z","updated_at":"2023-08-30T07:59:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This issue is meant for a central place for our Fortran (almost exclusively F77) codebase to track per module and if possible remove depending on contributor affinity to the subject matter and fortran-fu. 

List has projected effort required just based on code length hence involves prejudice.

- [ ] `scipy.integrate`:
  -  [ ] `dop `Convert: Easy
  - [ ] `mach` Convert: Easy (helper funcs used in `odepack` and `quadpack`)
  - [ ] `odepack`: Convert : Hard
  - [ ] `quadpack`: Convert : Moderate-Hard
- [ ] `scipy.interpolate`
  - [ ] `fitpack`: Convert : Moderate-Hard (modernized f90 implementation https://github.com/perazz/fitpack)
- [ ] `scipy.io`
  - [ ] Test Fortran : ? 
- [ ] `scipy.linalg`
  - [x] `lu.f`: Replaced in #18358
  - [x]  `det.f`: Replaced in #18225
  - [x] `id_dist`: Convert: Moderate (replacement in progress) #18367
  - [ ] `blas`: Convert: Very Hard (Depends on BLAS/LAPACK lib) A lot of historical wrapper inconsistencies
  - [ ] `lapack`: Convert : Very Hard (Depends on BLAS/LAPACK lib) A lot of historical wrapper inconsistencies
- [ ] `scipy.odr`
  - [ ] `odrpack`: Convert : Moderate
- [ ] `scipy.optimize`
  - [x] `nnls`: Replaced #18570
  - [ ] `cobyla` : Convert : Very Hard (The new implementation PRIMA is going to replace cobyla)
  - [ ] `L-BFGS-B`: Convert : Hard
  - [ ] `minpack`: Convert : Hard (LFortran team is active on this one too)
  - [ ] `minpack2`: Replaced in #19060
  - [ ] `slsqp`: Replaced in #19121
- [ ] `scipy.sparse.linalg`
  - [ ] ARPACK Convert : Hard
  - [x] `iterative`: Replaced in #18488
  - [ ] PROPACK : Convert : Hard
- [ ] `scipy.special`
  - [ ] `AMOS`: Convert : Hard
  - [ ] `cdflib`: Convert : Hard
  - [ ] `mach`: Convert : Easy (helper funcs)
  - [ ] `specfun`: Convert: Very Hard
- [x] `scipy.stats` 
  - [x] `statlib`: Replaced in #18679 
  - [x] `mvndst`: Already implemented in #17410 but not public yet

A few tests serving as examples can be found here: https://github.com/fortran-lang/minpack/blob/main/python/minpack/test_library.py

But I guess this doesn't really solve any of the problems you have with F77 which are:
- easily installing a Fortran compiler on all OS's
- the constraints the legacy codes impose when it comes to more feature rich and Pythonic interfaces
- lack of developers interested in learning Fortran
- the fact NumPy defaults to row-major

It appears easier for people to create something new in their own (closed) community than to work across programming languages and tools.

Some time ago I rewrote NNLS with modern Fortran flow-control constructs in an attempt to make it more maintainable and easy to interface. Unfortunately, I cannot live off of refactoring Fortran codes, hence I had no motivation to contribute it to SciPy, despite being a user.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/minpack/issues/78","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/minpack","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/minpack/issues/78/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/minpack/issues/78/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/minpack/issues/78/events","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/minpack/issues/78","id":1788107725,"node_id":"I_kwDOGFHczs5qlFvN","number":78,"title":"Do C callbacks require extern "C"?","user":{"login":"ivan-pi","id":21085643,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDg1NjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/21085643?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi","html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-07-04T15:05:22Z","updated_at":"2023-07-04T15:05:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Assuming the [C interface](https://github.com/fortran-lang/minpack/blob/main/include/minpack.h) of MINPACK is used in C++ program, should the callback types also have C linkage specified?
```cpp
extern "C" {                  // <-- do we need this?
typedef void (*minpack_func)(
    int /* n */,
    const double* /* x */,
    double* /* fvec */,
    int* /* iflag */,
    void* /* udata */);
}                             // <-- do we need this?
```

The last section of this [Oracle document](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19059-01/wrkshp50/805-4956/bajdcjch/index.html), _Pointers to Functions as Function Parameters_, appears to say that unless the linkage is specified, the following declaration
```cpp
MINPACK_EXTERN void MINPACK_CALL
minpack_hybrd(
    minpack_func /* fcn */,
    int /* n */,
    double* /* x */,
    double* /* fvec */,
    ...);
```
assumea the `fcn` argument will have C++ linkage and not C, like expected by Fortran. 

Typically, C and C++ compilers of the same vendor will be binary compatible when it comes to function linkage, but in principle they don't have to be. request"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2022-03-12T18:58:05Z","updated_at":"2023-07-04T15:31:39Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently, we are using double precision. Consider having the option of:
1. exporting multiple precisions
2. specify the precision via preprocessor flag

For 2, I usually would do something like this:

```fortran
#ifdef REAL32
    integer,parameter,public :: minpack_rk = real32   !! real kind used by this module [4 bytes]
#elif REAL64
    integer,parameter,public :: minpack_rk = real64   !! real kind used by this module [8 bytes]
#elif REAL128
    integer,parameter,public :: minpack_rk = real128  !! real kind used by this module [16 bytes]
#else
    integer,parameter,public :: minpack_rk = real64   !! real kind used by this module [8 bytes]
#endif

    integer,parameter,private :: wp = minpack_rk  !! local copy of `minpack_rk` with a shorter name
```

So, if you do nothing, you get double precision. The module also doesn't export `wp`, since that is too short a variable name to be exporting (if you want it, you will get `minpack_rk`).

For 1: Look at what I did for [quadpack](https://github.com/jacobwilliams/quadpack/tree/master/src). Not sure we need that for minpack though. setting","user":{"login":"perazz","id":11460684,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNDYwNjg0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11460684?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/perazz","html_url":"https://github.com/perazz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/perazz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/perazz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/perazz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/perazz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/perazz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/perazz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/perazz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/perazz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/perazz/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-07-04T15:21:33Z","updated_at":"2023-07-04T15:42:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/pulls/952","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/fpm/pull/952","diff_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/fpm/pull/952.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/fpm/pull/952.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"I'm opening a PR to address #950. 

The request is to enable dependencies to have user-specified pre-processor macros. 

I suggest to do it via the package manifest, under the `[dependencies]` section: each dependency can have an _optional_`preprocess` node with the same contents and structure as the one in the package manifest: 

```toml
[dependencies]
utils = { path = "crate/utils" , preprocess.cpp.macros=["DEPENDENCY_MACRO"] }
```

Seems a bit verbose, but I think it's important that the same syntax for preprocessing is used here or in the package manifest (BTW - cpp is the only supported thus far...)                                                                                                                

Of course, it is the user's responsibility to ensure that user-enabled macros do not clash with package-level macros, there is no way to check that.

request"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2023-06-25T03:48:22Z","updated_at":"2023-07-04T15:48:42Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Description

Fortran's default floating point precision is single precision, and the most used data type in scientific computing is floating point.

There are quite a few libraries (e.g. [slsqp](https://github.com/jacobwilliams/slsqp/blob/master/src/slsqp_kinds.F90)) that provide macro-based precision options, which seems very useful when developers don't want to write multiple precision versions of routines and polymorphic interfaces.

Is it possible to have macro definitions for dependency libraries in top-level projects, e.g.:

```toml
[features]
slsqp = ['REAL32']
minpack = ['REAL32']
M_stopwatch = ['_WIN32']
```

This also has a positive effect on the fpm library ecology.

### Possible Solution

Style 1:

```toml
[dependencies]
slsqp.git = 'https://github.com/jacobwilliams/slsqp'
slsqp.tag = '1.4.1'
slsqp.features = ['REAL32']
```

Style 2:

```toml
[dependencies]
slsqp = { git = 'https://github.com/jacobwilliams/slsqp', tag = '1.4.1', features = ['REAL32'] }
```

Style 3:

```toml
[features]
slsqp = ['REAL32']

[dependencies]
slsqp.git = 'https://github.com/jacobwilliams/slsqp'
slsqp.tag = '1.4.1'
```


### Additional Information

- [cargo::features](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/features.html)

request"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2023-06-25T03:48:22Z","updated_at":"2023-07-04T16:18:45Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Description

Fortran's default floating point precision is single precision, and the most used data type in scientific computing is floating point.

There are quite a few libraries (e.g. [slsqp](https://github.com/jacobwilliams/slsqp/blob/master/src/slsqp_kinds.F90)) that provide macro-based precision options, which seems very useful when developers don't want to write multiple precision versions of routines and polymorphic interfaces.

Is it possible to have macro definitions for dependency libraries in top-level projects, e.g.:

```toml
[features]
slsqp = ['REAL32']
minpack = ['REAL32']
M_stopwatch = ['_WIN32']
```

This also has a positive effect on the fpm library ecology.

### Possible Solution

Style 1:

```toml
[dependencies]
slsqp.git = 'https://github.com/jacobwilliams/slsqp'
slsqp.tag = '1.4.1'
slsqp.features = ['REAL32']
```

Style 2:

```toml
[dependencies]
slsqp = { git = 'https://github.com/jacobwilliams/slsqp', tag = '1.4.1', features = ['REAL32'] }
```

Style 3:

```toml
[features]
slsqp = ['REAL32']

[dependencies]
slsqp.git = 'https://github.com/jacobwilliams/slsqp'
slsqp.tag = '1.4.1'
```


### Additional Information

- [cargo::features](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/features.html)

Then everyone in the fpm ecosystem could all just depend on fpm:

```toml
[dependencies]
fpm = { git = 'https://github.com/fortran-lang/fpm' }
```

```fortran
module mymod
use fpm_kinds, only: wp => fpm_wp
```

In addition we could implement special preprocessing of the `fpm_kinds` module and have a fpm flag `--prec-model=4/8/16` (standard gfortran choice), --prec-model=1/2/3 (NAG flags). NAG Fortran has somewhat related flags such as `-kind=sequential` (the default), `-kind=byte` or `-kind=unique` to select representation methods. Now when we have physical types for arrays, it's the `ISODescriptorArray` of `array_physical_type`, so we just have to provide casts, and then some C header file I think.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/1966/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/1966/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/1630279194","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/1966#issuecomment-1630279194","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/1966","id":1630279194,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc5hLBYa","user":{"login":"ivan-pi","id":21085643,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDg1NjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/21085643?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi","html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-07-11T07:16:31Z","updated_at":"2023-07-11T07:16:31Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"The position of the first three struct members (`base_addr`, `elem_len`, and `version`) is prescribed by the standard. The `dim` member must come last.

That's a multi-faceted question. The vendor-specific attributes are probably meaningless because we don't know what purpose they serve. You can replace them with padding.

In the threads mentioned, Peter Klausler, Jeff Hammond, and Steve Lionel, had the opinion that it's too late to achieve ABI compatibility, because vendors are unlikely to change, and Fortran modules are incompatible anyway, so we usually compile from source. In HPC it's still common to build from source (e.g. with Spack) for performance tuning reasons, so ABI problems matter less I guess. Our center for examples provides modules compiled with Intel and GCC. (We have Intel Skylake Xeon.)
Also in the engineering and scientific industries that use Fortran, I think it's common to stick to one compiler for shipping the final software app. 

On personal systems, users like to install software in binary format and forget about it. A stable ABI is then an advantage, because you can rely on your system package manager. So on most Linux distros this would be GCC; that also applies to Python packages using Fortran. I guess most scientific Python users don't install the Intel OneAPI toolkit or the NVIDIA HPC SDK. NAG is out of the equation for non-institutional clients.

So my suggestion would be to seek for symbiosis with gfortran or flang, if you think that makes sense.

We need to turn standard-semantics on with ifort, because lhs reallocation is not enabled by default otherwise. That means that most of the code ought to be rewritten (allocatable character variaboles).

To solve that issue you would need just the `-assume realloc_lhs` flag. It appears like there is no `-nostandard-semantics` flag. One needs to "opt in" to all of the non-standard behaviors using [`-assume`.](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/docs/fortran-compiler/developer-guide-reference/2023-0/assume-002.html#GUID-DAEBF260-BE29-4CBD-9417-F774597F9071).

Motivation

Functions for low-order polynomial fitting using (weighted) least squares regression are common in many scientific libraries. 

Here's an interface for the non-weighted case, inspired by the numpy version cited below:

```
  function polyfit(x, y, deg, rcond, rank, singular_values) result(p)
    real, intent(in) :: x(:), y(:)
    integer, intent(in) :: deg
    real, intent(in), optional :: rcond
    integer, intent(out), optional :: rank
    real, intent(out), allocatable, optional :: singular_values(:)
```

### Prior Art

#### Prior art in popular scripting languages:
* [`numpy.polyfit`](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.polyfit.html)
* [`polyfit` (MATLAB)](https://de.mathworks.com/help/matlab/ref/polyfit.html)
* [`Polynomials.polyfit` (Julia)](https://juliamath.github.io/Polynomials.jl/v0.4/#Polynomials.polyfit)
* [`lm` (R)](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/stats/versions/3.6.2/topics/lm) (in R one would use a combination of lm for linear regression and [poly](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/stats/versions/3.6.2/topics/poly) as demonstrated [here](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/stats/versions/3.6.2/topics/poly))

#### Prior art in Fortran (in no particular order):
* [`POLFIT`](https://www.osti.gov/biblio/4190768-polfit-program-polynomial-least-squares-fitting) - A 704 program for polynomial least squares fitting (Fortran II)
* [`LSQFT` - A nonlinear least squares data fitting subroutine suitable for minicomputers](https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/5254362/)
* [SLATEC: `dpolft`](https://www.netlib.org/slatec/src/dpolft.f) - Fit discrete data in a least squares sense by polynomials (related routines include [`dpcoef`](https://www.netlib.org/slatec/src/dpcoef.f) and [`dp1vlu`](https://www.netlib.org/slatec/src/dp1vlu.f))
* [IMSL Fortran Library: `RCURV`](https://help.imsl.com/fortran/6.0/math/default.htm?turl=usagenotes3.htm) (also see [`poly_regression`](https://www.imsl.com/blog/fitting-polynomials-data-using-imsl-c) in the C interface)
* [NAG E02 Curve and Surface Fitting](https://www.nag.com/numeric/nl/nagdoc_latest/flhtml/e02/e02intro.html) (see routines [`e02adf`](https://www.nag.com/numeric/nl/nagdoc_latest/flhtml/e02/e02adf.html) and [`e02aef`](https://www.nag.com/numeric/nl/nagdoc_latest/flhtml/e02/e02aef.html))
* [polyfit - Toolbox for performing and manipulating polynomial fits to data with statistical uncertainties.](https://github.com/plopezrios/polyfit)
* [Least-Squares Polynomial Curve-Fitting Utilizing Orthogonal Polynomials](https://ttu-ir.tdl.org/bitstream/handle/2346/12980/31295001405587.pdf?sequence=1) (thesis by Robert E. Knight, contains Fortran code)
* [FITLOS: A FORTRAN program for fitting low-order polynomial splices by the method of least squares](https://www.pdas.com/refs/tnd6401.pdf)
* [An Evaluation of Linear Least Squares Computer Programs](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/jres/73B/jresv73Bn2p59_A1b.pdf) (article by Roy H. Wampler from National Bureau of Standard, lists over 20 codes, many in Fortran)
* [`spfit`](http://www.netlib.org/math/docpdf/ch11-01.pdf) (part of [MATH77/mathc90](http://www.netlib.org/math/) library, available on Netlib)
* [Polyfit program users' guide : a statistical polynomial curvefitting program](https://rosap.ntl.bts.gov/view/dot/10020)
* [curvefit](https://github.com/jchristopherson/curvefit)
* [E201 Least Squares Polynomial Fit](https://cmd.inp.nsk.su/old/cmd2/manuals/cernlib/shortwrups/node113.html) (part of CERNLIB)
* [Numerical Algorithms in Fortran](http://www.gbv.de/dms/ilmenau/toc/213842300.PDF) by Gisela Engeln-Müllges & Frank Uhlig (see Chapter 8 on linear and nonlinear approximation)
* [`LS_POLY`](http://jean-pierre.moreau.pagesperso-orange.fr/f_lstsqr.html) routine by Jean-Pierre Moreau (part of the `LSQPLY.F90` file)
* [PROGRESS: a program for robust regression](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/0165993688900222?via%3Dihub)
* [IBM Scientific Subroutine Package](http://media.ibm1130.org/1130-106-ocr.pdf) (some Fortran source files are available [here](https://github.com/msemina/SSP))

#### Prior art in other programming languages
* [GSL](https://www.gnu.org/software/gsl/doc/html/lls.html): Linear Least-Squares Fitting (can be used via the Fortran interface [fgsl](https://github.com/reinh-bader/fgsl), the relevant routine is called `multifit` which may be used for any linear model)
* [alglib](https://www.alglib.net/interpolation/leastsquares.php) (C++)

### Additional Information

This will probably require LAPACK for the factorization of the Vandermonde matrix either using a rank-revealing QR or SVD factorization.

A function to evaluate the polynomial (and it's derivatives) using Horner's scheme (or alternatively, in some orthogonal form), should be added in parallel. 

An advanced interface could consider using derived types for polynomials, similar to the numpy [Polynomial](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/routines.polynomials.html) sub-package.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/601/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/601/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/comments/1449593133","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/601#issuecomment-1449593133","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/601","id":1449593133,"node_id":"IC_kwDODZiDws5WZwkt","user":{"login":"ivan-pi","id":21085643,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMDg1NjQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/21085643?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi","html_url":"https://github.com/ivan-pi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ivan-pi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-03-01T08:49:12Z","updated_at":"2023-03-01T08:49:12Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I We need a few more voices to move this issue forward. Given how many polynomial fitting codes are out there, it looks like polynomial regression is quite popular among practitioners.

Do you prefer the simple interface or the complex one?

```fortran
    ! simple
    subroutine polyfit(x,y,n,p)
        real(wp), intent(in) :: x(:)
        real(wp), intent(in) :: y(:)
        integer(ip), intent(in) :: n
        real(wp), intent(out) :: p(:)
    end subroutine

    ! complex
   subroutine polyfit(x, y, deg, p, rcond, rank, singular_values)
      real(wp), intent(in) :: x(:), y(:)
      integer(ip), intent(in) :: deg
      real(wp), intent(out) :: p(:)
      real(wp), intent(in), optional :: rcond
      integer(wp), intent(out), optional :: rank
      real(wp), intent(out), allocatable, optional :: singular_values(:)
   end subroutine
```

How do you guard against that?

In principle you could parse the contents of [`compiler_options()`](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gfortran/COMPILER_005fOPTIONS.html#COMPILER_005fOPTIONS) intrinsic function and issue a warning to the `error_unit`, say if `-ffast-math` was detected with gfortran.

But in general I'd just go with a warning in the documentation.

module reverse_module
  
    implicit none
    private
    public :: reverse_tmpl, test_reverse
  
    requirement default_behavior(T)
      type :: T; end type
    end requirement
  
    template reverse_tmpl(T)
      requires default_behavior(T)
      private
      public :: reverse
    contains
        subroutine swap(x, y)
            type(T), intent(inout) :: x, y
            type(T) :: tmp
            tmp = x
            x = y
            y = tmp
        end subroutine reverse

        subroutine reverse(array)
            type(T), intent(inout) :: array(:)
            integer :: i, j
        
            do i = 1,size(array)/2
                j        = size(array) + 1 - i
                call swap(array(i), array(j))
            end do
        end subroutine reverse
    end template
    
contains

  subroutine test_reverse()
      instantiate reverse_tmpl(integer), only: irev => reverse
      integer :: a(5)
      a = [1,2,3,4,5]
      call irev(a)
      print *, a
  end subroutine
  
end module

  program main
  use reverse_module
  call test_reverse()
  end program
```

   |            ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^...
...

32 |            end subroutine reverse

suppose a simple unit test could look something as follows:
```
program test_get_os_type

!$ use omp_lib, only: omp_get_num_threads, omp_get_thread_num, &
!$    omp_get_max_threads
   use fpm_environment, only: get_os_type
   implicit none

   integer, allocatable :: os(:)
   integer :: n, i

   n = 1
!$ n = omp_get_max_threads()
   print *, "num_threads = ", n

   allocate(os(0:n-1))

   i = 0
!$omp parallel
!$ i = omp_get_thread_num()
   os(i) = get_os_type()
!$omp end parallel

   print *, all(os == os(0))

end program
```

The results I get:
```
$ fpm test test_get_os_type --flag -fopenmp
 num_threads =            4
 T

```

From a quick glance at [CI.yml](https://github.com/fortran-lang/fpm/blob/main/.github/workflows/CI.yml) I don't think the parallel build features are being tested currently.

A few questions related to this:
- Does the bootstrap fpm require parallel features?
- Would it suffice to add another _parallel_ test target? This would be similar to the line: https://github.com/fortran-lang/fpm/blob/4be07afeeeebd79f36cb3f62a10c06415c0e24f6/.github/workflows/CI.yml#L148

my sincere apologies for this very late draft. 

I am in Paderborn at the deRSE23 and will be giving a 15-min talk about Fortran-lang at 12:00 (at noon, in 5h 30min). If you have any suggestions I should be able to integrate until 10:00 AM

The organizers expect us to use their laptop for the presentation, so I decided against doing any kind of demo.

The talk is in the session _Education and Digital literacy_. In the talk I plan to stir things in that direction.

All errors are mine and mine alone.
